text: Achieving global cultural sustainability is an increasingly challenging task, demanding a careful balance between the preservation of local cultural identity and the promotion of innovation. The role of design in achieving this balance is investigated in the context of the concept of cosmopolitan localism, which implies that design can achieve a balance between the protection of intangible cultural heritage of a place and its insertion in global networks of ideas. This paper thus explores, through a two-level research design, the underlying relationships between design strategy, social innovation and sustainable branding. Based on this theoretical framework, two design strategies in China are analyzed as examples of how a design-based approach can help social enterprises transform local resources into sustainable intangible cultural products. The paper draws the conclusion that design can act as a mediator that articulates global and local values and provides the formal and stylistic tools that are necessary to address the urgent cultural issues of the time. A strategic design approach can therefore be seen as a highly relevant approach in dealing with the complex issues that we encounter on a daily basis in our modern lives, while at the same time preserving the cultural heritage of a local area in a dynamic way.
